questions.questionText: A remote-controlled car has a mass of 1.5 kg. It accelerates from rest to 6.0 m/s in 3.0 seconds. Calculate the resultant force acting on the car.

commonErrors: Using velocity (6.0) instead of acceleration (2.0) in F = ma

questions.questionText: An object has a 5 N force acting upwards and a 5 N force acting downwards. What happens to the object?
options: It remains stationary or moves at constant velocity
It moves upwards at constant speed then stops

markScheme.answer: It remains stationary or moves at constant velocity

steps.description: Zero resultant means no change in motion

commonErrors: Thinking balanced forces always mean the object is stationary

questions.questionText: Explain why an astronaut's weight is different on the Moon compared to on Earth, but their mass stays the same.

markScheme.answer: Mass is the amount of matter, which does not change with location. Weight depends on gravitational field strength (W = mg). The Moon has a weaker gravitational field than Earth, so the weight is less.

steps.description: Mass is the amount of matter and is constant everywhere
The Moon has a weaker gravitational field strength, so weight is smaller

commonErrors: Saying there is no gravity on the Moon

questions.questionText: A person pushes a wheelbarrow 20 m using a force of 50 N. Calculate the work done.

A 500 kg bumper car travelling at 4 m/s collides head-on with a 600 kg bumper car travelling at 3 m/s in the opposite direction. They stick together after the collision. Calculate their velocity after the collision and state the direction.

markScheme.answer: 0.18 m/s in the direction of the first car

steps.description: v = 0.18 m/s in the direction of the first car

commonErrors: Not assigning opposite signs for opposite directions

questions.questionText: A parachutist jumps from a plane. Describe how the forces on the parachutist change from the moment they jump until they reach terminal velocity (before opening the parachute).

markScheme.answer: Initially, weight is much greater than air resistance, so the resultant force is large and the parachutist accelerates quickly. As speed increases, air resistance increases. The resultant force decreases so acceleration decreases. Eventually air resistance equals weight, the resultant force is zero and terminal velocity is reached.

steps.description: At first, weight >> air resistance, so there is a large resultant downward force and the parachutist accelerates
As speed increases, air resistance increases, reducing the resultant force and reducing acceleration
When air resistance = weight, resultant force is zero and terminal velocity is reached

commonErrors: Not explaining that air resistance increases with speed
Saying forces are balanced from the start
